coredata.dc:title: Influence of interactions between bubbles on physico-chemical effects of acoustic cavitation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Dui
Lei, Shuang
Zhang, Bingyu
Liu, Yanping
Tian, Jian
Ji, Xiaojuan
Yang, Haiyan
coredata.dc:description: Ultrasound technology has been extensively used as one of the efficient and economic methodology to achieve the desired outcomes in many applications by harnessing the physico-chemical effects of acoustic cavitation. However, the cavitation-associated effects, primarily determined by the oscillatory dynamics of cavitation bubbles, are considerably complex and still remain poorly understood. The main objective of this study was to perform a numerical analysis of the acoustic cavitation (i.e., the cavitation dynamics, the resultant temperature, pressure and chemical yields within collapsing bubbles), particularly focusing on the influence of the interactions between bubbles. A comprehensive model was developed to simulate the acoustic cavitation dynamics via combining the influences of mass transfer, heat conduction and chemical reactions as well as the interaction effects between bubbles. The results demonstrated that only the large bubble exerts a greater impact on the small one in a two-bubble system. Specifically, within parameter ranges covered this study, there are noticeable decreases in the expansion ratio of the small bubble, the resultant temperature, pressure and molar yields of free radicals, hence weakening the cavitation intensity and cavitation- associated physico-chemical effects. Moreover, the influences of the interactions between bubbles were further assessed quantitatively under various parameters, such as the ultrasound amplitude PA and frequency f, the distance between bubbles d 0, the initial radius of the large bubble R 20, as well as the liquid properties (e.g., surface tension σ and viscosity μ). It was found that the suppression effect can be amplified when subjected to ultrasound with an increased PA and/or a decreased f, probably due to a stronger cavitation intensity under this condition. Additionally, the suppression effect is also enhanced with a decrease in d 0, σ and μ, but with R 20 increasing. This study can contribute to deepening knowledge about acoustic cavitation and the resultant physical and/or chemical effects, potentially further facilitating the ultrasound-assisted various applications involving acoustic cavitation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Acoustic cavitation
Cavitation dynamics
Physico-chemical effects
Interactions between bubbles

head.ce:title: Influence of interactions between bubbles on physico-chemical effects of acoustic cavitation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biomedical Engineering, School of Bioinformatics, Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications, Chongqing, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biomedical Engineering, School of Bioinformatics, Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications, Chongqing, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Postdoctoral Workstation of Chongqing General Hospital, Chongqing, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Postdoctoral Workstation of Chongqing General Hospital

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Ultrasound, Chongqing General Hospital, Chongqing, People’s Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Ultrasound, Chongqing General Hospital, Chongqing, People’s Republic of China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: No. 2 Chongwen Road, Nan'an District, Chongqing 400065, People’s Republic of China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultrasound technology has been extensively used as one of the efficient and economic methodology to achieve the desired outcomes in many applications by harnessing the physico-chemical effects of acoustic cavitation. However, the cavitation-associated effects, primarily determined by the oscillatory dynamics of cavitation bubbles, are considerably complex and still remain poorly understood. The main objective of this study was to perform a numerical analysis of the acoustic cavitation (i.e., the cavitation dynamics, the resultant temperature, pressure and chemical yields within collapsing bubbles), particularly focusing on the influence of the interactions between bubbles. A comprehensive model was developed to simulate the acoustic cavitation dynamics via combining the influences of mass transfer, heat conduction and chemical reactions as well as the interaction effects between bubbles. The results demonstrated that only the large bubble exerts a greater impact on the small one in a two-bubble system. Specifically, within parameter ranges covered this study, there are noticeable decreases in the expansion ratio of the small bubble, the resultant temperature, pressure and molar yields of free radicals, hence weakening the cavitation intensity and cavitation- associated physico-chemical effects. Moreover, the influences of the interactions between bubbles were further assessed quantitatively under various parameters, such as the ultrasound amplitude PA and frequency f, the distance between bubbles d 0, the initial radius of the large bubble R 20, as well as the liquid properties (e.g., surface tension σ and viscosity μ). It was found that the suppression effect can be amplified when subjected to ultrasound with an increased PA and/or a decreased f, probably due to a stronger cavitation intensity under this condition. Additionally, the suppression effect is also enhanced with a decrease in d 0, σ and μ, but with R 20 increasing. This study can contribute to deepening knowledge about acoustic cavitation and the resultant physical and/or chemical effects, potentially further facilitating the ultrasound-assisted various applications involving acoustic cavitation.

ce:section.ce:para: Acoustic cavitation refers to a complex dynamic process in which gas or/and vapor-filled microbubbles are formed in liquids under sufficiently intense ultrasonic excitation and then these bubbles undergo growth, oscillation and ultimately collapse [1,2]. Due to the violent bubble collapse, a series of unique physical and chemical phenomena can occur during acoustic cavitation, such as the local high temperature, high pressure, sonoluminescence, shockwaves, high-speed jets [3–6] and the production of highly reactive free radicals within violently collapsing bubbles [7–9]. By harnessing these physical and chemical effects associated with cavitation, acoustic cavitation has found extensive applications for achieving the desired outcomes in the chemical industry and biomedical fields, e.g., the ultrasonic cleaning [10], sonolysis [11,12], extraction [13], emulsification [14], distillation [15], nanosynthesis [16], as well as the ultrasound imaging and therapy [9,17,18] to mention a few. Understanding the transient cavitation bubble dynamics and the associated physico-chemical effects is crucial for optimizing these various applications in different areas. However, the micrometer scale, high-speed vibration and complex cavitation effects of acoustic cavitation bubbles considerably increase the difficulty of experimental investigation. Alternatively, some researchers focus on the theoretical research of acoustic cavitation based on modelling and simulation, which has provided additional insight into the complex acoustic cavitation characteristics.

With respect to the theoretical investigation on acoustic cavitation, the cavitation dynamics of a single bubble has attracted considerable attention and has been extensively studied up to now. Originally, Rayleigh was the first to propose the dynamic model of an ideal spherical cavitation bubble in infinite incompressible liquid [19]. Subsequently, some researchers modified the cavitation model to simulate the single bubble dynamics with introducing the effects of liquid properties, such as surface tension and viscosity [20–22]. Further advances in modelling have incorporated the compressibility of the surrounding liquid, the acoustic radiation effect and the pressure-dependent sound velocity to provide a more accurate description of the cavitation bubble dynamics [23–26]. More recently, the cavitation bubble dynamics have been numerically investigated using a comprehensive model that includes the non-equilibrium evaporation and condensation of water vapor, the thermal effects, as well as the chemical reactions [7,27–29]. It was found that the cavitation dynamics and physico-chemical effects can be affected by these three energy mechanisms to various degrees under different operating conditions. Moreover, mass transport, heat conduction, and chemical reactions have been identified as important factors to consider while modelling and numerically investigating the acoustic cavitation. Based on different models, these studies have elucidated the transient dynamic process of a single cavitation bubble at the microsecond and micrometer scales. Meanwhile, the influences of mass transfer, heat conduction and chemical reactions on the dynamic behavior of the bubble and the cavitation effects have also been revealed, which is crucial for various applications benefitting from the physico-chemical effects associated with acoustic cavitation.

In contrast to the single bubble case, cavitation bubbles in real liquid systems are typically presented as bubble groups or bubble clouds, resulting in a more complicated cavitation dynamics due to the interactions between bubbles. To gain fundamental insights into the effects of the inter-bubble interactions on the dynamic behaviors of cavitation bubbles, researchers often focus on a two-bubble system for the sake of simplicity. For instance, the influence of the interactions between bubbles on the radial oscillations of a two-bubble system has been studied. It was manifested that the interactions between bubbles can notably reduce the radial oscillations of the smaller bubble [30–33]. In contrast, recent studies have shown that interactions between bubbles can have not only a suppressing effect but also an enhancing effect on bubble dynamics [30,33–37]. Apart from the radial bubble oscillations, Doinikov [38] investigated the translational motions of a two-bubble system in water by combining the velocity potential formula. It was found that the interactions between bubbles can induce the bubbles to attract or repel each other, leading to a relatively stable state after a certain time. Moreover, Qin et al. [34] examined the nonlinear dynamics and the acoustic radiation characteristics of two-bubble systems under various driving conditions, taking into account both the translational and radial motions of the bubbles. They found that in the interacting two-bubble system, the larger bubble tends to have a much stronger impact on the smaller one. In addition to the cavitation dynamics, the influences of the interactions between bubbles on the mechanical effects of acoustic cavitation have also been investigated in a two-bubble system [39]. The results indicated that the interactions between bubbles can decrease the compressive and tensile stresses arising from the cavitation bubble dynamics process. It should be noted that the interactions between bubbles were investigated without considering the mass transfer, heat conduction and chemical reactions in all aforementioned studies. Up to now, there has been little quantitative investigation into the dynamics of acoustic cavitation and the resultant cavitation effects in dual-/multi-bubble systems via simultaneously considering the impacts of mass transfer, heat conduction and chemical reactions.

In this work, a comprehensive model was developed to simulate the acoustic cavitation dynamics, with simultaneously coupling the effects of mass transfer, thermal conduction and chemical reactions as well as the interaction effects between bubbles. The main aim of this study was to quantitatively assess the impacts of interactions between bubbles on the cavitation dynamics, as well as the physical and chemical effects associated with acoustic cavitation. Specifically, the modified Keller–Miksis equations were coupled with Kalmar’s chemical model ELTE/HD2022 to simulate the acoustic cavitation dynamics of two-bubble systems having different initial bubble sizes (R 10 and R 20). Then, the acoustic cavitation characteristics, including the bubble expansion ratio as well as the temperature, pressure and chemical yields within the bubble, were assessed quantitatively. Furthermore, the influences of the interactions between bubbles on the acoustic cavitation characteristics were examined within various parameters (i.e., the ultrasound amplitude PA and frequency f, the distance between bubbles d 0, the initial size of the large bubble R 20, the surface tension σ and liquid viscosity μ).

A numerical model of a two-bubble system was developed by incorporating the impacts of the mass transfer, heat conduction and chemical reactions. The oscillating bubbles are initially composed of water vapor (H2O) and non-condensable oxygen gas. Based on the previous studies [40–44], the microbubbles are assumed to remain spherical during acoustic cavitation and do not fragment after collapse [41]. Accordingly, the non-spherical oscillations and fragmentation of bubbles are ignored in the present study, similar to previous studies [41,43,45]. Moreover, the temperature, pressure and concentration of each substance inside the microbubbles are assumed to be spatially uniform.

The modified Keller–Miksis equation has been widely used to describe the dynamics of cavitation bubble in sonochemistry [30,34,46]. Accordingly, the radial dynamics in a two-bubble system were described by utilizing the modified Keller–Miksis equations that are coupled with the interactions between bubbles, which are given by Lauterborn and Kurz [47]:

where Ri and Rj denote the radii of the i-th (i = 1, 2) and j-th bubbles (j = 3−i), respectively. The dots represent the time derivative, c and ρ are the sound and the density of the liquid, respectively. pw,i is the liquid pressure at the i-th bubble interface, and d 0 is the distance between bubbles. It is noticed that the equation can be used to describe the radial dynamics of a single bubble when the right-hand side of Eq. (1) is equal to zero.

According to pressure equilibrium conditions, the liquid pressure pw,i is of the following form:

are the critical temperatures and pressures of water vapor and oxygen, respectively. Their values can be readily retrieved in [50].

The mass transfer includes the evaporation/condensation of water vapor [4,29,51] and the rectified diffusion of non-condensable gases [52–54]. Previous research has shown that the evaporation and condensation of water vapor have a greater impact than the rectified diffusion of non-condensable gases [29,51,55,56]. Therefore, this study focuses solely on the evaporation/condensation of water, disregarding the rectified diffusion effect. The mass flux of water vapor at the i-th bubble interface are given as follows [4,57,58]:
(7)

Here,
m ̇
eva , i
and

con , i
are the rates of evaporation and condensation at the wall of the i-th bubble, respectively. αM is the accommodation coefficient,
p
vap , i
sat
and
p
H 2
O , i
are the saturated vapor pressure and actual vapor pressure in the i-th bubble, respectively,
W
H 2
O
is the mass weight of water, Rvap is the gas constant of water, and T∞ is the ambient temperature. Note that the accommodation coefficient αM is used to estimate the resistance to condensation at the bubble wall during bubble collapse [4]. A smaller value of αM means stronger phase-change resistance, leading to a significant increase in the amount of water retained within the collapsing bubble. Previous studies have reported various values for αM , ranging from 0 to 0.35, 0.4 and so on [7,57,59–61]. In this study, the value of αM is set to 0.35, consistent with the value used by Kalmar et al [29].

Similar to the mass transport, the amount of thermal conduction at the interface of the i-th bubble and the thickness of thermal boundary layer Lth are calculated as [29]:

where Ai is the surface area of the i-th bubble, λi and κi are the coefficients of thermal conductivity and diffusivity of the i-th bubble (here and below, the overline on each variable represents its averaged value of the mixture), respectively, and their expressions are written as:

is the total concentrations of the species. Cp is molar heat capacity at the constant pressure, ρ is the density, and W is the molecular weight. The values of λk and W are retrieved in [29]. Specifically, the average values of the mixture inside the i-th bubble can be expressed as the weighted average of the components based on respective mole fractions, which are given by:

For the bubble containing oxygen and water vapor initially, we utilized Kalmar’s chemical model ELTE/HD2022 [29], which considered 34 chemical reactions during acoustic cavitation. The kinetic mechanisms of the chemical reactions have been extensively described in [29,62–66], therefore, we will provide a brief description here for the sake of conciseness. Alternatively, readers can also refer to [29] for a more comprehensive description of the chemical reactions model, including the NASA coefficients and Arrhenius-constants used in our simulations.

The general form of the chemical species (O, H•, H2, •OH, O2, H2O, HO2•, H2O2, O3) involved in these reversible reactions are expressed as follows:

Furthermore, the temperature inside the i-th bubble Ti is estimated through the solution of the time-dependent form of the first law of thermodynamics:

The physical parameters used in the simulations and their default values unless specified otherwise are given in Table 1 . The Runge-Kutta method is employed in MATLAB software (MathWorks Inc., R2022b) to numerically solve ordinary differential equations. Specifically, the system of ODEs was solved using the ODE15s solver of MATLAB with the absolute and relative tolerances set to 1 × 10-10.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of interaction effects on acoustic cavitation characteristics
ce:section.ce:para:
Previous studies have demonstrated that the adjacent bubble can distinctly affect the bubble oscillations due to the interactions between bubbles, even though the mass transport, thermal conduction and chemical reactions were neglected when modelling the bubble dynamics in these studies [30,31,34,38]. Therefore, we first sought to evaluate how the interactions between bubbles affect the bubble radius, temperature and pressure within the bubble with taking into account the mass transfer, heat conduction and chemical reactions simultaneously. A comparison was conducted between the two cases with or without considering the interaction effects, including the cavitation dynamics (i.e., the relative radius (
R
i
′
=
R i
t
/
R
i 0
), the internal temperature (
T i
) and the pressure (
P i
) of the i-th bubble) of a two-bubble system.

As indicated in Fig. 1 (a)–(c), the two-bubble system presented noticeable reductions in the expansion ratio (
R
1 , m a x
′
/
R 10
, defined as the maximum value of the relative radius), T 1 and P 1 for the small bubble when compared to the single bubble model (R 1-w versus R 1-w/o). In contrast, one can find from Fig. 1(d)–(f) that there seem to be negligible changes for the large bubble (R 2-w versus R 2-w/o). Accordingly, these results suggest that the cavitation dynamics of the small bubble are affected to a greater degree by the interactions between bubbles, as compared to the large one. These findings align with recent studies that investigated the interaction effects on bubble oscillations without taking the mass transport, thermal conduction and chemical reactions into account [30,67,68]. More specifically, it might be concluded that the dynamics of the largest bubble can dominate and control the oscillations of smaller bubbles within the dual-, multi-bubble systems [30].

Moreover, it can be seen from Fig. 1 that the Ti and Pi for both bubbles increase sharply at the end of bubble collapse phase. Under these extreme conditions, chemical reactions occur within the bubbles, consequently leading to generation of radicals and other products. Fig. 2 presented the time evolution of the reaction products within the bubbles (
n
k , i
), including O, H•, H2, •OH, HO2•, H2O2 and O3, for two cases with (w) or without (w/o) taking the interactions between bubbles into account. It is evident that the molar yield of each free radical gradually increases over time, following the same trend as the Ti and Pi . Specifically, the higher the temperature and pressure are, especially for the cases at the moment of bubble collapse, the higher the molar yields of free radicals get.

For the influences of interactions between bubbles on the chemical yields, it is obvious that the molar yield of each component within the small bubble is distinctly reduced (Fig. 2(b)), while that within the large bubble is not significantly changed (Fig. 2(d)), as compared to the cases of isolated bubble (Fig. 2(a) and (c)) respectively. There is ample evidence that the influences of interaction effects on the chemical yields accord well with the cavitation dynamics shown in Fig. 1. More importantly, from the findings depicted in Figs. 1 and 2, it might be inferred that the interactions between bubbles can greatly reduce the acoustic cavitation intensity and consequently weaken the resultant high temperature, high pressure and/or chemical yields inside the cavitation bubble. Additionally, these results also draw our attention to the importance of considering the interactions between bubbles while investigating the dynamic behaviors of cavitating bubbles and the associated physico-chemical effects of acoustic cavitation. In the following sections, we primarily focus on the expansion ratio, temperature, pressure, and total chemical yields of the small bubble while considering the inter-bubble interactions with a nearby large bubble, since it is evident that only the small bubble is affected by the nearby large bubble in two-bubble systems.

It is well known that the ultrasound amplitude PA and frequency f play a decisive role in the cavitation dynamics of a single bubble [43,69]. In a two-bubble system with varying initial radii, the impacts of PA on the cavitation dynamics of the small bubble were examined within a range of PA = 1.2–2 atm, as shown in Fig. 3 . It can be seen that the maximum expansion ratio
R
max
′
, as well as the maximum temperature
T
max
, the maximum pressure
P
max
and the total chemical yields
n
tot
= ∑
n
k , i
(i.e., the sum of the chemical yields involving the aforementioned seven species in Fig. 2) within the bubble increase with an increase in PA . It indicates that the cavitation intensity is remarkably enhanced at a higher PA , accordingly resulting in stronger physico-chemical effects associated with acoustic cavitation.

Moreover, as compared to the case of a single bubble model, obvious suppression effects act on the
R
max
′
,
T
max
,
P
max
and
n
tot
when the interaction effects are considered. It can be expected that the differences between the two cases are amplified as PA increases, due to the stronger bubble–bubble interaction at a larger PA . In accordance with the present results, previous studies have demonstrated that the radial oscillations of a small bubble would be noticeably suppressed while interacting with a large bubble, particularly at higher acoustic pressures [34,70,71]. Specifically, the higher the acoustic pressure is, the stronger the bubble–bubble interaction becomes. It should be noted that a remarkable reduction in the chemical yields is caused by the interaction effects as PA is sufficiently high (Fig. 3(d)), which further highlights the need to consider the interaction effects from nearby bubbles for certain applications in sonochemistry where high acoustic pressures are applied.

Fig. 4 compared the acoustic cavitation characteristics of the small bubble at different ultrasound frequencies f with or without considering the interactions between bubbles. Within ultrasound frequencies ranging from 20 to 200 kHz, it is obvious that the cavitation dynamics of the small bubble is reduced, exhibiting an evident decrease in
R
max
′
. Consequently, this can result in a distinct reduction of the
T
max
and
P
max
, concomitantly inhibiting the chemical reactions and then diminishing the production of free radicals. These results could be attributed to the fact that the high-frequency ultrasound having a shorter time-period of negative acoustic pressure allows the bubble to expand continuously over a shorter duration, consequently leading to a smaller bubble expansion ratio and weaker cavitation intensity. Similar variation tendency has been investigated in the previous study which focused on the role of interactions between bubbles on radial pulsations [33]. Moreover, combining Fig. 3(d) and Fig. 4(d), it can be found that the maximum value of
n
tot
is achieved at the maximum value of PA and the minimum value of f. This finding is in well agreement with that reported by Kalmar et al.[60], who investigated the relationship between the radial bubble dynamics and the chemical yields. The optimal conditions for achieving high chemical yields have been extensively studied and surprising results have been presented for a single bubble case [51,56]. It was demonstrated that chemical yields have an optimum around the resonance frequency of the bubble. This is dependent on operating conditions and species. In the present study, we aim to investigate the influences of interactions between two bubbles at various simulation conditions and further elucidate the relationships between the influences of interactions and the simulation parameters. Due to the inter-bubble interactions, it can be expected that determining the optimum operation conditions in two-bubble systems will be more complicated than the single bubble case, which is beyond the scope of this paper. Nonetheless, we recognize the vital importance and practical value of achieving optimum conditions for chemical yields and plan to address this issue during acoustic cavitation of bubbles in the future as part of an ongoing extension of this study.

In addition, for the interaction effects, it can be obvious in Fig. 4 that within all frequency parameters examined here, all of the
R
max
′
,
T
max
,
P
max
and
n
tot
can be prominently reduced owing to the interactions between bubbles. Further analysis shows that a much stronger suppression effect can be observed at a lower f, probably due to the stronger cavitation intensity under this condition. Taken together, this result is in well agreement with that presented in Fig. 3, i.e., the suppression effect of the interactions between bubbles is enhanced with increasing cavitation intensity at a higher PA and/or a lower f.

It has previously been observed that the interaction effects in a two-bubble system not only strongly depend on the ultrasound parameters, but also are highly dependent on the distance between bubbles and the ambient radius of the nearby bubble [33,34,39,71]. Hence, using the two-bubble model established in the present study, the acoustic cavitation characteristics of a small bubble (R 10 = 2 µm) as a function of the distance between bubbles (d 0 = 80–300 µm) and the ambient radius of a nearby large bubble (R 20 = 2–10 µm) were further examined, as shown in Fig. 5 .

Under all simulation parameters examined in this study, the differences in
R
max
′
,
T
max
,
P
max
and
n
tot
between the interacting and non-interacting cases are negative, which are respectively defined as
Δ X =
X w
-
X
w / o
. Here, X denotes the observed variable. These findings further verify the fact that the interactions between bubbles with a large bubble could noticeably restrain the expansion of the small bubble and accordingly weaken the physico-chemical effects of acoustic cavitation. Obviously, it can be seen that the suppression effect becomes weaker and weaker as d 0 increases and/or R 20 decreases. These results align with previous observations that considered the interaction effect on bubble dynamics while neglecting its impact on the physical and chemical effects associated with acoustic cavitation [30,34,72–75]. Furthermore, when comparing the degrees of differences with respect to the variations of d 0 and R 20 respectively, it is apparent that the influences of the interactions between bubbles on the acoustic cavitation characteristics seem to be much stronger with an increase in R 20 rather than d 0. Taken together, these findings indicate that the interaction effects from a large bubble acting on the cavitation dynamics of a small bubble should be seriously considered and investigated, especially for the bubble systems having large differences in bubble sizes and small bubble-to-bubble distances.

The properties of the liquids surrounding the cavitation bubbles (e.g., surface tension σ and viscosity μ) can also affect the acoustic cavitation dynamics. To further investigate their effects on the small bubble of the two-bubble system, the results obtained with or without considering the interactions between bubbles were compared, as displayed in Fig. 6 .

Similar to the results displayed in Fig. 5, it is obvious from Fig. 6 that the differences in the bubble expansion ratio Δ
R
max
′
, the maximum temperature ΔTmax , the maximum pressure ΔPmax and the total chemical yields Δntot between two cases are also negative. More importantly, it is evident in Fig. 6(a) that at a smaller σ and/or a smaller μ, the interactions between bubbles have a more pronounced suppression effect on the bubble expansion ratio. Furthermore, these results also indicate that increasing σ and/or μ could reduce the influence of interaction between bubbles on the physico-chemical effects of acoustic cavitation, as displayed in Fig. 6(b)−(d). These findings might be explained by the fact that the cavitation dynamics of bubbles can be reduced with σ and/or μ increasing [55,76–78], thereby weakening the influence of inter-bubble interactions exerting on each other.

The main objective of the current study was to examine the influence of the interactions between bubbles on the acoustic cavitation characteristics in a two-bubble system, including the radial dynamics of cavitation bubbles, and the high temperature, high pressure as well as the chemical yields generated during the collapse phase. A comprehensive model was developed to simulate the complex acoustic cavitation of two-interacting bubbles, taking into account the effects of mass transfer, thermal conduction, chemical reactions, and interaction effects simultaneously. The results clearly indicated that in the two-bubble system, the interaction effects markedly restrain the radial oscillations of the small bubble while exerting a negligible effect on the large bubble within the parameter ranges examined in this study. Specifically, the obvious suppression effect could diminish the bubble expansion ratio and consequently reduce the resultant temperature, pressure and chemical yields within the small bubble, indicative of an evident decrease in the cavitation intensity and the physico-chemical effects of acoustic cavitation. Moreover, the influences of interactions between bubbles were found to be strongly correlated with the key parameters related to the driving ultrasound, the two-bubble system and the surrounding liquid. It was demonstrated that the interaction effects can be notably increased with an increase in ultrasound amplitude and/or a decrease in ultrasound frequency. With regards to the parameters of the two-bubble system, stronger interactions between bubbles were observed with smaller bubble-to-bubble distances and larger ambient radii of nearby bubbles. Additionally, it could also be found that increasing the surface tension and liquid viscosity can reduce the influence of the interactions between bubbles on the acoustic cavitation characteristics. Overall, this study quantitatively assessed the influences of interactions between bubbles on the cavitation dynamic behaviors and the physico-chemical effects of acoustic cavitation, further highlighting the importance of considering the influences of the interactions between bubbles in particular for certain application scenarios involving strong acoustic cavitation.

This study might provide important insights into the interactions between bubbles and the resultant influences on the cavitation effects, but this work clearly has some limitations. First, previous studies have shown that the influence of the interactions between bubbles on the bubble dynamics can be either suppressed or enhanced, depending on some factors such as ultrasound parameters, bubble sizes and so on [30,33–37]. However, a suppression effect is always observed within the simulation parameters tested in the present study, thus further investigation is needed to clarify the complex inter-bubble interactions over much wider parameter ranges. Second, cavitation bubbles are assumed to remain spherical and do not fragment after collapse. Actually, the non-spherical oscillations and fragmentation of bubbles may occur in certain cases. Third, the effects of time delay and nonlinear wave propagation are neglected. It is crucial to consider these effects in future studies to ensure more accurate simulations of the bubble dynamics. Finally, two-bubble systems are used to describe the interactions between bubbles. Further research needs to examine the interaction effects more comprehensively among much more cavitation bubbles.

Dui Qin: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Shuang Lei: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Methodology. Bingyu Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Yanping Liu: Validation, Software. Jian Tian: Validation, Funding acquisition. Xiaojuan Ji: Resources, Conceptualization. Haiyan Yang: Resources, Project administration, Conceptualization.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effects of the non-equilibrium condensation of vapour on the pressure wave produced by the collapse of a bubble in a liquid
